figures.caption: Figure 3: t-SNE diagram of latents (MNIST, DZ = 20) learned by the diffusion encoder. Each figure shows the equilibrium distribution q⋆(z|x) of Eqs. (21) and (38), at a given temperature γ. As γ increases, stochasticity dominates in Eq. (21), scrambling the latent clusters into the prior p(z).
Figure 6: Rate-distortion curves and reconstructions with a VAE for MNIST and CIFAR-10. Reconstructions were generated with DZ = 20 for MNIST, and DZ = 128 for CIFAR-10.
Figure 4: Rate-Distortion curves for a VAE and dAE (diffusion encoder + conv. decoder).

Figure 2: A toy model illustrating the limitations of a Gaussian encoder (cf. Eq. (8)). Left: Samples from the true joint density p(x, z) (blue) and the learned one (red). Middle: The true posterior at two values of x, and the Gaussian encoder that best fits it. Right: The true marginal density and the one reconstructed by the VAE. The Gaussian encoder is unable to capture the true posterior at any x, which restricts the decoder to be a mostly linear function of z. See also Fig. 9.
Figure 7: Tiny ImageNet training and reconstructions with a diffusion encoder + convolutional decoder (dAE), and latent dimensions DZ = 512.

Figure 5: Rate-distortion curves and reconstructions with a diffusion encoder + convolutional decoder (dAE) for MNIST and CIFAR-10. RD curves for different latent dimensions DZ are shown. Reconstructions were generated with DZ = 20 for MNIST, and DZ = 128 for CIFAR-10.
Figure 10: A schematic of the forward and reverse diffusion processes.
Figure 8: Reconstructions of CelebA-HQ (rescaled to 64× 64) with a diffusion encoder + convolutional decoder (dAE). Originals/reconstructions appear in odd/even rows.

Figure 9: A dAE applied to the toy model from Sec. 2. Left: Samples from the true joint density p(x, z) (blue) and the learned one (red). Middle: The true posterior at two values of x, and the diffusion encoder that best fits it. Right: The true marginal density and the one reconstructed by the dAE. The dAE admits more of the quadratic term ψ2z 2 from the polynomial decoder, which is why dψ(z) is ‘more symmetric’ under z → −z compared to Fig. 2. The learned posteriors are also nearly symmetric, just like p(z|x), but they still have a roughly Gaussian silhouette. This is even though the diffusion encoder is fully capable of capturing p(z|x). See Sec. 6 for discussion.
